sources.title: Age- and sex-dependent clinical equations to estimate glomerular filtration rates in children and young adults with chronic kidney disease
sources.organization: Kidney International (Elsevier / International Society of Nephrology)
limitations: Internal validation only (CKiD subset), no external validation performed by the authors
Population is mild-to-moderate CKD (median mGFR ~48); not validated in healthy children or for screening
sCr-based equations require patient height
supports: The U25 equations take the form eGFR = K x (height/serum creatinine) or eGFR = K x (1/cystatin C), reported in mL/min per 1.73 m2, with two variants (sex-dependent K, or sex-and-age-dependent K) per biomarker. (clm_051).
The sex-specific (age-independent) creatinine K constant is 41.8 for males and 37.6 for females. (clm_052).
In the age-dependent creatinine model, K rises monotonically from age 1 to 18 then stays constant to 25; males 35.7 (age 1) to 50.8 (>=18), females 33.1 to 41.4. (clm_053).
The constant cystatin C K is 81.9 for males and 74.9 for females (eGFR = K x 1/cysC, cysC in mg/L). (clm_054).
In the age-dependent cystatin C model K is non-monotonic, peaking at age 15 for males (K=87.2, rising from 74.8) and age 12 for females (K=79.9, rising from 76.5). (clm_055).

The K constants are assay-specific: serum creatinine was assayed enzymatically on an Advia 2400 (Siemens) and IDMS-standardized, and cystatin C nephelometrically (Siemens) and calibrated to IFCC standards; using differently calibrated assays can invalidate the constants. (clm_056).

Developed/internally validated on 2655 iohexol-mGFR observations from 928 CKiD participants; among 13 equations compared, only the two U25 age-dependent equations achieved non-significant bias and P30 accuracy >85% in both children (<18 y) and young adults (>=18 y); the equations contain no race coefficient. (clm_057).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Age- and sex-dependent clinical equations to estimate glomerular filtration rates in children and young adults with chronic kidney disease — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2021

sources.title: Self-reported Race, Serum Creatinine, Cystatin C, and GFR in Children and Young Adults With Pediatric Kidney Diseases: A Report From the Chronic Kidney Disease in Children (CKiD) Study
sources.organization: American Journal of Kidney Diseases (National Kidney Foundation) / Elsevier
limitations: All participants have a CKD diagnosis; may not generalize to children free of kidney disease
Small subgroup <6 years (n=46 person-visits)
Estimated lean body mass proxy; no directly measured muscle mass or genetic ancestry
Abstract and Results body report slightly different cohort counts (see Limitations)
supports: In internal validation, the U25 creatinine-only eGFR significantly underestimated measured GFR in self-reported Black participants (bias -3.37 mL/min/1.73 m2), whereas the cystatin C-only U25 eGFR showed no significant bias across race groups. (clm_068).
In unadjusted models, self-reported Black race was associated with 12.8% higher measured GFR after adjusting for serum creatinine. (clm_069).
Adjusting for cystatin C, self-reported Black race was associated with 3.5% lower measured GFR overall, an effect that attenuated and became non-significant in older age groups. (clm_070).

The average of the creatinine-based and cystatin C-based U25 equations yielded unbiased GFR estimates and had the highest proportions within 30% and within 10% of mGFR and the lowest RMSE among the three U25 forms. (clm_071).

Measured GFR was obtained by plasma iohexol disappearance and BSA-standardized with the Haycock formula; serum creatinine (mg/dL) and cystatin C (mg/L) were centrally measured with IFCC-calibrated methods. (clm_072).

The analytic cohort comprised 190 self-reported Black participants (473 person-visits) and 675 non-Black participants (1,897 person-visits), median age 9 years; median SCr was 1.2 mg/dL in both groups while median cystatin C and mGFR differed by group. (clm_073).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Self-reported Race, Serum Creatinine, Cystatin C, and GFR in Children and Young Adults With Pediatric Kidney Diseases: A Report From the Chronic Kidney Disease in Children (CKiD) Study — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2021

sources.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for Screening and Management of High Blood Pressure in Children and Adolescents
authors: Subcommittee on Screening and Management of High Blood Pressure in Children
limitations: Static adolescent cut points (>=13y) adopt adult 2017 ACC/AHA thresholds; not derived from pediatric outcome data (subcommittee retained a statistical, not outcome-based, definition).
Normative percentile tables derived from auscultatory data; oscillometric/ABPM devices may yield different values.
conflictsWith: 2004 Fourth Report (superseded; different staging and 'prehypertension' terminology, tables included overweight/obese children)
European (ESH) and Hoshide/other national pediatric BP definitions retain percentile-only schemes for adolescents rather than static adult cut points
supports: The guideline replaces the term 'prehypertension' with 'elevated blood pressure', aligning pediatric terminology with the 2017 AHA/ACC adult guideline. (clm_031).

For children aged 1 to <13 years, BP categories are percentile-based with a static-mmHg upper bound applied by 'whichever is lower': Normal <90th pct; Elevated >=90th to <95th pct (or 120/80); Stage 1 >=95th to <95th+12 mmHg (or 130/80-139/89); Stage 2 >=95th+12 mmHg (or >=140/90). (clm_032).
For adolescents >=13 years, BP is classified by static adult-style mmHg cut points: Normal <120/<80; Elevated 120/<80 to 129/<80; Stage 1 130/80-139/89; Stage 2 >=140/90 mm Hg. (clm_033).
HTN is diagnosed when a child/adolescent has auscultatory-confirmed BP readings >=95th percentile at 3 different visits (grade C, moderate). (clm_034).
Left ventricular hypertrophy is defined as LV mass >51 g/m2.7 (boys and girls) for children/adolescents older than 8 y, and as LV mass >115 g/BSA for boys and >95 g/BSA for girls. (clm_035).
New normative BP tables are based on normal-weight children only; the 50th/90th/95th percentiles were derived by quantile regression on normal-weight children (BMI <85th percentile). (clm_036).

BP should be measured annually in children and adolescents >=3 y of age, and at every health care encounter for those with obesity, BP-raising medications, renal disease, aortic arch obstruction/coarctation, or diabetes (both grade C, moderate). (clm_037).

sources.title: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease
authors: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) CKD Work Group
sources.organization: Kidney International (Elsevier) — official journal of the International Society of Nephrology
limitations: Guideline scope is primarily adult with an explicit pediatric-considerations subsection; it is not a dedicated pediatric reference-interval source.
Table 1 (CKD diagnostic criteria, incl. the '<60 mL/min/1.73 m2' defining threshold) renders as a graphic and was not extractable as verbatim text from the PDF.
conflictsWith: KDIGO 2012 CKD guideline (used a 60 mL/min/1.73 m2 'low GFR' cutoff for children; 2024 raises it to 90)
supports: KDIGO 2024 defines CKD as abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for a minimum of 3 months with implications for health, applied to children and adults. (clm_008).
For children and adolescents over age 2, KDIGO 2024 advises flagging eGFRcr <90 mL/min/1.73 m2 as 'low' — a higher cutoff than the adult threshold and than the 2012 pediatric cutoff of 60. (clm_009).
KDIGO GFR categories span G1 (>=90) to G5 (<15) mL/min/1.73 m2, with G3 split into G3a and G3b; these category boundaries are population-general staging, not the pediatric 'low' flag. (clm_010).
KDIGO albuminuria categories are A1 <30 mg/g, A2 30-300 mg/g, A3 >300 mg/g by urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (with mg/mmol equivalents). (clm_011).

KDIGO 2024 directs that GFR in children be estimated with equations validated in comparable populations, naming the CKiD U25 2021 creatinine equation (ages 1-25; uses age, sex, height) as a preferred example. (clm_012).

KDIGO 2024 flags creatinine-assay dependence in children: labs must QC the lowest end of the expected range, and enzymatic (not Jaffe) creatinine assays are preferred because non-creatinine chromogens contribute more in children and neonatal samples are frequently icteric/hemolyzed. (clm_013).
sources.surveillanceQuery: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2024

sources.title: Utility of Cystatin C-based Equation for the Estimation of Glomerular Filtration Rate in a Pediatric Population
sources.organization: The Journal of Applied Laboratory Medicine (Oxford University Press / AACC)
limitations: Small single-center cohort (N=57)
Abstract-only access — full Results/Methods tables not verifiable
Performance metrics are cohort-specific, not universal clinical cutoffs
supports: In this pediatric/young-adult cohort the average measured GFR (mGFR by Tc-99m DTPA) was 84.1 mL/min/1.73 m2. (clm_063).
Non-race-corrected creatinine equations overestimated eGFR in all groups; CKiD U25-creatinine had the lowest bias at 22.59 mL/min/1.73 m2. (clm_064).

CKiD U25-CysC and Schwartz CysC gave the best correlation, P30, and lowest bias; CKiD U25-CysC = correlation 0.6281, P30 80.7%, bias 3.72 mL/min/1.73 m2, and Schwartz CysC = 0.6372, 77.2%, -4.68 mL/min/1.73 m2. (clm_065).
mGFR was measured by intravenous Tc-99m DTPA tracer and retrospectively compared with equation-based eGFR in 57 patients aged 6 months to 22 years of different races/ethnicities. (clm_066).
The authors conclude that both CKiD U25-CysC and Schwartz CysC estimate mGFR well, with CKiD U25-CysC having the overall best performance in this cohort. (clm_067).

sources.title: Comparison of different equations for estimating the glomerular filtration rate in pediatric kidney transplant recipients
limitations: Small cohort (n=23 patients, 120 samples), single center
No measured-GFR gold standard — comparisons are equation-vs-equation
Thai cohort with common post-transplant growth retardation may not generalize
Reference CKiD 2012 equation derived in CKD stage >=3 children, may not reflect near-normal-GFR transplant recipients
supports: ROC analysis identified eGFR-equation agreement thresholds vs the CKiD 2012 reference of <70 mL/min/1.73 m2 for Schwartz-Lyon, U25, and FAS-height, and <60 mL/min/1.73 m2 for Bedside Schwartz, beyond which bias increased. (clm_019).
Among plasma-creatinine-only equations, Schwartz-Lyon had the highest agreement with the CKiD 2012 reference (ICC 0.913, CCC 0.911, TDI 14.0 mL/min/1.73 m2, P30 99.2%). (clm_020).

The U25 combined PCr-CystC equation showed excellent agreement with the CKiD 2012 combined reference (ICC 0.993, CCC 0.990, TDI 3.9 mL/min/1.73 m2, P30 100%), and is preferred where cystatin C is feasible. (clm_021).
The plasma-creatinine-only U25 equation achieved ICC 0.922, CCC 0.882, TDI 20.0 mL/min/1.73 m2, and P30 93.3% against the CKiD 2012 combined reference. (clm_022).

Plasma creatinine was measured by an enzymatic method and cystatin C by a particle-enhanced immunoturbidimetric assay, applied throughout the study — a documented assay-method dependence for any derived threshold. (clm_023).

The study cohort comprised 120 eGFR measurements from 23 pediatric kidney transplant recipients (mean age 14.2 +/- 3.4 years) who received adult-donor kidneys (donor mean age 31.7 +/- 10.0 years). (clm_024).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Comparison of different equations for estimating the glomerular filtration rate in pediatric kidney transplant recipients — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2025

sources.title: Hematuria and Proteinuria in Children
sources.organization: American Academy of Pediatrics (Pediatrics in Review)
limitations: Definitions are review-level syntheses; some ranges (e.g., microscopic hematuria 1-10 RBC/HPF in the literature) are explicitly heterogeneous
mg/m2 superscripts were lost in machine text extraction and reconstructed from the published print convention
supports: The authors define microscopic hematuria as >5 RBC/mm3 uncentrifuged or >5 RBC/HPF centrifuged on at least 2-3 occasions, while noting literature definitions range 1-10 RBC/HPF. (clm_025).
Persistent hematuria is defined as >4-6 weeks of UA showing >5 RBC/HPF absent exercise, menses, or trauma. (clm_026).
Hypercalciuria in children >2 years is a urine calcium/creatinine ratio >0.2 mg/mg; infant acceptable thresholds are higher (<0.8 under 6 months, <0.6 for 6-12 months). (clm_027).
Proteinuria is defined as >100 mg/m2 per day or U p/c >0.2 mg/mg on a spot sample; neonates/infants allowed up to 300 mg/m2, and normal U p/c is <0.5 mg/mg for infants 6-24 months. (clm_028).
Nephrotic-range proteinuria is defined as >1,000 mg/m2 per day, or >50 mg/kg per day, or a spot U p/c >2. (clm_029).

Orthostatic proteinuria is diagnosed when a first-morning U p/c is <=0.2 mg/mg with a random-sample U p/c >0.2 (or positive dipstick); it is one of the most common causes of proteinuria in adolescents and has a benign course. (clm_030).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Hematuria and Proteinuria in Children — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2018

sources.title: Proteinuria in Children: Evaluation and Differential Diagnosis
sources.organization: American Academy of Family Physicians (American Family Physician)
supports: Urine dipstick color grades map to protein concentrations trace=15, 1+=30, 2+=100, 3+=300, 4+>=1,000 mg/dL, and a reading of 1+ or more is considered abnormal. (clm_001).
A spot first-morning urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 0.2 or less (0.5 or less at six to 24 months of age) with normal urinalysis indicates likely transient or orthostatic proteinuria. (clm_002).
In children the normal amount of protein excreted is less than 100 mg per m2 per day on 24-hour quantitation. (clm_003).
Nephrotic-range (heavy) proteinuria is defined as more than 1,000 mg per m2 per day, or a urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of more than 2. (clm_004).
A high urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (>2 in nonglomerular disease, >0.5 in glomerular disease) predicts significant CKD progression in children. (clm_005).
Depressed GFR is characterized as less than 60 mL per minute per 1.73 m2 for more than three months and is among the criteria prompting pediatric-nephrology referral and renal biopsy. (clm_006).

Orthostatic proteinuria is the most common type in children (especially adolescent males) and is benign; referral and biopsy are reserved for red-flag features such as active sediment, hematuria, hypertension, hypocomplementemia, or depressed GFR. (clm_007).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Proteinuria in Children: Evaluation and Differential Diagnosis — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2017

sources.title: Evaluation of Proteinuria and Hematuria in Ambulatory Setting
limitations: Full text paywalled (no PMC copy; Elsevier returned 403) — only the published abstract was accessible verbatim, so numeric cutoffs stated in the body could not be locator-verified and are logged as GAPs.
Narrative review — thresholds it cites are themselves derived from other guidelines/sources.
supports: Proteinuria in children can be glomerular and/or tubular in origin and is temporally categorized as transient, orthostatic, or persistent. (clm_045).
Persistent proteinuria may indicate serious underlying kidney pathology and warrants further evaluation. (clm_046).
Hematuria is defined as an increased number of red blood cells in the urine and may be gross or microscopic. (clm_047).
Hematuria in children can originate from the glomeruli or from other sites in the urinary tract. (clm_048).
Asymptomatic isolated microscopic hematuria or mild proteinuria in an otherwise healthy child is less likely to be clinically significant. (clm_049).
The co-occurrence of both hematuria and proteinuria requires further workup and careful monitoring. (clm_050).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Evaluation of Proteinuria and Hematuria in Ambulatory Setting — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2022

sources.title: Application of GFR estimating equations to children with normal, near-normal, or discordant GFR
limitations: Small sample (n=29)
Normal/near-normal GFR range only
Full text not accessible; body-of-paper numbers not verifiable here
supports: In 29 children with mild CKD, 22 had creatinine- vs cystatin-C-based eGFR that differed by at least 15 mL/min/1.73 m2, establishing that threshold as the study's definition of clinically meaningful discordance. (clm_058).
The CKiD U25 equation most accurately identified children with an eGFR below 90 mL/min/1.73 m2, the threshold this study used to flag low/near-normal GFR. (clm_059).

The direction of discordance dictated which equation best matched measured GFR: when creatinine-eGFR exceeded cystatin-C-eGFR by at least 15 mL/min the U25 creatinine formula was closest to 4-point iohexol GFR, and when cystatin-C-eGFR was higher the U25-combined was closest. (clm_060).

The authors recommend the CKiD U25-combined (creatinine + cystatin-C) formula to screen children for a low GFR, and either U25-combined or FAS-combined for tracking eGFR change longitudinally. (clm_061).

All six equations disagreed with 4-point measured iohexol GFR in more than one-third of participants, showing pediatric eGFR formulas remain unreliable at the normal/near-normal range and need refinement. (clm_062).
sources.surveillanceQuery: Application of GFR estimating equations to children with normal, near-normal, or discordant GFR — pediatric nephrology/CKD update after 2023

sources.title: Diagnostic utility of protein to creatinine ratio (P/C ratio) in spot urine sample within routine clinical practice
sources.organization: Taylor & Francis (Critical Reviews in Clinical Laboratory Sciences)
limitations: Full text not accessible (HTTP 403 / paywall); pediatric-specific numeric thresholds not present in the abstract
Narrative review, not a primary diagnostic-accuracy study or reference-interval derivation
supports: A spot urine P/C ratio above 20 mg/mmol (0.2 mg/mg) is the most commonly reported cutoff for detecting proteinuria. (clm_014).
A spot urine P/C ratio above 350 mg/mmol (3.5 mg/mg) confirms nephrotic-range proteinuria. (clm_015).
The ISSHP recommends a P/C ratio of 30 mg/mmol (0.3 mg/mg) to classify proteinuria in pregnant women at risk of preeclampsia. (clm_016).
The P/C ratio cutoff should be established per laboratory because it depends on the patient population and the laboratory methodologies. (clm_017).
A high degree of correlation was observed between spot P/C ratio values and protein concentration in 24-hour urine collections, supporting P/C as a substitute for 24-h collection. (clm_018).

sources.title: Comparison of estimated GFR using cystatin C versus creatinine in pediatric kidney transplant recipients
limitations: Single center, n=45, selection bias (biopsy-triggered enrollment)
Cohort skewed to mGFR>60; poor generalizability to advanced CKD
CysC assay/equation heterogeneity limits head-to-head comparison
Full-text-to-markdown conversion dropped several equation exponents (see Limitations)
conflictsWith: de Souza et al. (CysC formulas not superior to Cr in pediatric Tx on low-dose steroids)
Selistre et al. (Cr-based preferred in pediatric Tx)
supports: In 45 pediatric kidney transplant recipients aged 1-18, median measured (iohexol) GFR was 93.3 mL/min/1.73 m2, with 57.8% >=90, 33.3% between 60-89, and 8.9% between 30-59. (clm_038).
Among the whole cohort, the U25-CysC equation had the highest accuracy, with 88.9% of estimates within 30% (P30) and 37.8% within 10% (P10) of measured GFR. (clm_039).
The Gentian CysC equation had the smallest mean bias in the whole cohort at 0.1 mL/min/1.73 m2 (IQR -16.6 to 12.9). (clm_040).
The CAPA equation misclassified K/DOQI CKD stage the least — 24.4% of the whole cohort and 33.3% of the histological-change subgroup — versus roughly 40-50% for most equations. (clm_041).
Bland-Altman analysis showed Bedside Schwartz, Gentian CysC, CAPA, and U25-CysC tended to overestimate GFR in patients with GFR above 100 mL/min/1.73 m2. (clm_042).
The study applied the Bedside Schwartz creatinine-based equation eGFR = 0.413 x (height/Cr), with height in cm and Cr in mg/dL. (clm_043).
CysC was measured by turbidimetric assay (Gentian AS) standardized to IFCC reference material; for the pre-IFCC CKiD Cr-CysC equation the Gentian CysC value was divided by 1.17. (clm_044).
